slides.title: Project Vision

Project vision slide.

Title: 'Project Vision / رؤية المشروع'
Kicker: 'WHY WE BUILD'

Left column (60%): The vision statement broken into 3 paragraphs (each 3-4 sentences, 60-100 words):

Para 1 (English heading 'Mission'): 'Build the fastest, most reliable food delivery platform in Egypt — engineered to handle 50,000+ daily orders at launch while keeping the operator cost structure lean enough to compete on commission with Talabat and elmenus. The platform serves 7 distinct user types through dedicated apps, all backed by a Go-powered event-driven backend that scales horizontally.'

Para 2 (Arabic heading 'الرسالة'): 'نبني منصة توصيل طعام مصرية متكاملة تجمع بين سرعة الأداء وبتكلفة تشغيل منخفضة. المنصة بتدعم 7 أنواع مستخدمين، كل واحد ليه تطبيق مخصص — من العميل للمندوب للمطعم للدعم الفني لقائد العمليات لبوابة الموظفين للمشرف الميداني. الـ backend مكتوب بـ Go عشان السرعة والـ concurrency.'

Para 3 (heading 'Why now'): 'Egyptian food delivery market is projected to reach $1.7B by 2027 with 12% YoY growth. Existing players are slow to innovate on driver economics and SME restaurant onboarding. Local payments (Vodafone Cash, InstaPay) are now mature enough to support sub-second checkout flows.'

Right column (40%): 4 stat cards in a 2x2 grid:
- 50K+ Orders/day target
- 7 Apps (one per user type)
- <2s API p95 latency
- 99.9% Uptime SLA

Use slide header '01 — PROJECT OVERVIEW' and page '04 / 50'.

Title: 'Goals & Non-Goals / الأهداف والحدود'
Kicker: 'SCOPE'

Two columns side by side, each a card:

LEFT CARD — 'Goals / الأهداف' (with success-green accent):
- Build production-grade platform serving 7 user types through dedicated apps
- Handle 50K+ orders/day with horizontal scaling from day one
- Sub-2-second API response time at p95 under peak load
- Local payments integration (Vodafone Cash, InstaPay, Fawry, card)
- Real-time driver matching in under 10 seconds average
- Built-in anti-fraud layer covering fake orders, promo abuse, driver collusion
- Internal employee portal with biometric auth and audit logging to prevent internal fraud
- Multi-city rollout ready (Cairo → Giza → Alexandria → Mansoura)

RIGHT CARD — 'Non-Goals / خارج النطاق' (with warning-amber accent):
- Not building a B2B SaaS for other food brands in v1 (white-label deferred to v2)
- Not supporting grocery / pharmacy in v1 (food only — expansion in Phase 4)
- Not building proprietary POS hardware (integrate with existing POS via API)
- Not building own mapping/routing (use Google Maps + OSRM)
- Not building drone/robot delivery (research-only, not in roadmap)
- Not supporting crypto payments in v1 (regulatory unclear in Egypt)

slides.title: Differentiators
slides.task_brief:
Three strategic differentiators slide.

Title: 'Strategic Differentiators / الميزات التنافسية'
Kicker: 'WHY WE WIN'
Subtitle: 'Three moats that compound over time — driver economics, lower merchant cost, and trust through anti-fraud.'

Three large pillar cards side by side:

PILLAR 1 — 'Driver Economics'
Icon: two_wheeler (cyan)
Heading: 'Lower Commission, Higher Driver Take-Home'
Body (3-4 sentences, 60-80 words): 'We charge restaurants 12-15% commission vs Talabat\'s 20-25% — freeing 5-8 points of margin that we redirect to drivers as higher per-order payouts and instant-cashout features. Drivers see transparent earnings breakdowns in real-time. This creates a sticky driver supply, which compounds into faster delivery times and better customer experience.'
Metrics tag: 'Driver payout: 75% of commission vs 55% industry'

PILLAR 2 — 'Lower Merchant Cost'
Icon: store (purple)
Heading: 'SME-Friendly Onboarding & Pricing'
Body: 'Restaurants pay zero onboarding fees, zero monthly subscription, and a flat 12-15% per-order commission. We provide a free tablet + POS integration kit. Menu updates are self-serve through the Restaurant Web App. SME restaurants (the long tail of Egyptian food) get the same treatment as chains — a category Talabat largely ignores.'
Metrics tag: 'Onboarding time: <48 hours vs 2-3 weeks'

PILLAR 3 — 'Trust Layer'
Icon: shield (success-green)
Heading: 'Native Anti-Fraud & Employee Accountability'
Body: 'Every order passes through an ML fraud-scoring layer before confirmation. The internal Employee Portal requires biometric auth (fingerprint/face) and writes an immutable audit log for every action — preventing the internal fraud patterns that plague large delivery ops. Customers see a Trust Score on every restaurant.'
Metrics tag: 'Target fraud rate: <0.3% of GMV'

- Auth Service

Each card with a small Go icon and primary accent color.

slides.title: Tech Stack
slides.task_brief:
Tech stack breakdown slide.

Title: 'Technology Stack / التقنيات المستخدمة'
Kicker: 'STACK'
Subtitle: 'Performance-first Go backend, React across all clients, PostgreSQL as the source of truth.'

6 categories in a 2-column x 3-row grid. Each category card has:
- Category name (heading)
- 3-4 tech items with small icons
- One-line rationale

Categories:

1. BACKEND — Performance-critical
   - Go 1.22 (chi/echo router, goroutines for concurrency)
   - gRPC for inter-service communication
   - Protocol Buffers for typed contracts
   Rationale: 'Go chosen for low-latency, high-concurrency — handles 50K orders/day on modest infra.'

2. FRONTEND — Cross-platform
   - React Native (Customer, Driver, Restaurant, Supervisor apps)
   - React + Vite (Support, Command Center, Employee Portal — web only)
   - Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui for design system
   Rationale: 'React knowledge reused across mobile + web — single team can ship to all 7 clients.'

3. DATABASE — Transactional + geospatial
   - PostgreSQL 16 (primary OLTP, partitioned by city)
   - PostGIS extension (geospatial queries for driver matching)
   - Redis 7 (cache, session, geospatial index via GEOADD)
   Rationale: 'PostgreSQL + PostGIS handles 95% of needs; Redis for sub-ms geospatial lookups.'

4. EVENT BUS — Async backbone
   - Apache Kafka (order events, driver location streams)
   - Schema Registry (Avro for typed events)
   - Kafka Streams / Flink (real-time aggregations)
   Rationale: 'Kafka decouples services and provides replay for fraud analytics + order history.'

5. INFRASTRUCTRE — Containerized
   - Kubernetes (EKS self-managed on AWS)
   - Docker (multi-stage builds, distroless images)
   - Terraform (IaC, multi-env)
   - ArgoCD (GitOps deploys)
   Rationale: 'K8s gives horizontal autoscaling per service — critical for peak-hour spikes.'

6. OBSERVABILITY — Three pillars
   - Prometheus + Grafana (metrics)
   - Loki (logs)
   - Jaeger / OpenTelemetry (traces)
   - Sentry (client-side errors)
   Rationale: 'Single observability stack across all 12 services and 7 clients.'

Slide header '03 — SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE' and page '13 / 50'.

slides.title: Data Flow — Order Placement
slides.task_brief:
Order placement data flow diagram (CSS-built, no SVG).

Title: 'Data Flow — Order Placement / تدفق البيانات — إنشاء الطلب'
Kicker: 'REQUEST LIFECYCLE'
Subtitle: 'From customer tap to order confirmed — the synchronous path under 2 seconds.'

Horizontal flow with 7 stages, each a card, connected by thin arrow divs:

1. CUSTOMER APP
   Action: User taps 'Place Order'
   → POST /orders

2. API GATEWAY
   Validates JWT, rate-limits, routes
   → forwards to Order Service

3. ORDER SERVICE (Go)
   - Validates cart + pricing
   - Checks inventory via Restaurant Catalog
   - Calls Fraud Service (sync, <100ms)
   - Creates Order (status: PENDING)
   → publishes OrderCreated event to Kafka

4. FRAUD SERVICE
   - Returns risk score 0-100
   - If >80 → blocks, refund
   - If 50-80 → manual review queue
   - <50 → proceed

5. PAYMENT SERVICE
   - Calls Vodafone Cash / InstaPay API
   - Holds payment for 30s
   - On success → publishes PaymentCaptured

6. NOTIFICATION SERVICE (async)
   - Pushes to Restaurant App
   - Restaurant accepts → RestaurantAccepted event

7. DELIVERY MATCHING
   - Subscribes to RestaurantAccepted
   - Finds nearest available driver via Redis GEO
   - Pushes to Driver App
   - Driver accepts → OrderConfirmed

Bottom note: 'Total p95 latency target: <2s for steps 1-5; steps 6-7 happen async in <10s.'

Slide header '03 — SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE' and page '14 / 50'.

slides.title: Event-Driven Backbone
slides.task_brief:
Event-driven backbone explanation slide.

Title: 'Event-Driven Backbone / العمود الفقري للحدث'
Kicker: 'KAFKA · EVENT-DRIVEN'
Subtitle: 'Why events instead of synchronous calls for cross-service communication.'

Two-column layout:

LEFT COLUMN — 'Why Event-Driven? / ليه Event-Driven؟'
3 cards stacked:

Card 1 — 'Decoupling / الفصل':
'Services don\'t need to know about each other. The Order Service publishes OrderCreated; the Notification, Analytics, and Loyalty services all consume independently. Adding a new consumer (e.g., a future Recommendations service) requires zero changes to the Order Service.'

Card 2 — 'Resilience / المرونة':
'If the Analytics service is down, orders still flow. Kafka buffers events for up to 7 days. Failed consumers catch up when they recover. No cascading failures — a critical property during peak hours when individual services may degrade.'

Card 3 — 'Replay / إعادة التشغيل':
'Events are immutable and retained for 30 days. We can replay the order stream into a new fraud-detection model, rebuild a corrupted read model, or backfill analytics. This is impossible with synchronous architectures.'

RIGHT COLUMN — 'Core Event Topics / الموضوعات الأساسية':
A list of 8 Kafka topics with their producers/consumers (monospace styling):

• order.created         [Producer: Order Service | Consumers: Fraud, Notification, Analytics]
• order.confirmed       [Producer: Delivery Matching | Consumers: Notification, Restaurant, Customer]
• order.cancelled       [Producer: Order/Support | Consumers: Payment (refund), Driver (unassign)]
• payment.captured      [Producer: Payment | Consumers: Order, Loyalty, Analytics]
• payment.failed        [Producer: Payment | Consumers: Order, Notification]
• driver.location       [Producer: Driver App | Consumers: Geo Service, Command Center (streaming)]
• driver.status_changed [Producer: Driver Mgmt | Consumers: Delivery Matching, Analytics]
• restaurant.menu_updated [Producer: Restaurant App | Consumers: Catalog, Search]

Slide header '03 — SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE' and page '15 / 50'.

Title: '12 Domain Services / 12 خدمة مصممة حسب النطاق'

Services:

1. Auth Service — 'JWT issuance, OAuth2, MFA, session management'
   Owns: auth_db | Go + Redis | 500 RPS

2. Restaurant Catalog — 'Restaurant master data, hours, service areas'
   Owns: restaurants_db | Go + PostGIS | 2K RPS read

3. Menu Service — 'Menu items, pricing, modifiers, availability'
   Owns: menus_db | Go + Postgres | 3K RPS read

4. Order Service — 'Order lifecycle, state machine, validation'
   Owns: orders_db | Go + Kafka producer | 1K RPS write

5. Payment Service — 'Vodafone Cash, InstaPay, card, refunds'
   Owns: payments_db | Go + vault | 500 RPS

6. Delivery Matching — 'Driver-customer matching, dispatch algorithm'
   Owns: dispatch_db | Go + Redis GEO | 200 RPS

7. Driver Management — 'Driver profiles, KYC, vehicle, earnings'
   Owns: drivers_db | Go + Postgres | 300 RPS

8. Geo/Tracking — 'Real-time GPS ingestion, ETA computation'
   Owns: Redis only | Go + Redis GEO | 50K updates/sec

9. Notification — 'Push, SMS, email, in-app'
   Owns: notifications_db | Go + Kafka consumer | 1K RPS

10. Fraud Detection — 'ML scoring, rule engine, manual review queue'
    Owns: fraud_db | Go + Python ML sidecar | 500 RPS sync

11. Promo/Loyalty — 'Coupons, campaigns, points, cashback'
    Owns: promos_db | Go + Postgres | 200 RPS

12. Analytics/Reporting — 'Aggregations, dashboards, exports'
    Owns: analytics_db (ClickHouse) | Go + Flink | Async

Slide header '04 — MICROSERVICES BREAKDOWN' and page '17 / 50'.

slides.title: Service Communication Patterns
slides.task_brief:
Service communication patterns slide.

Title: 'Communication Patterns / أنماط التواصل'
Kicker: 'SYNC vs ASYNC'
Subtitle: 'When to use gRPC vs Kafka vs WebSocket — picked per use case, not as a religion.'

3 cards in a row, each describing one pattern:

CARD 1 — 'Synchronous · gRPC / المتزامن'
Icon: bolt
Accent: cyan
Use cases:
• Order Service → Fraud Service (need risk score before confirming)
• Order Service → Payment Service (need confirmation before notifying restaurant)
• Customer App → API Gateway → any read query
Properties: 'Low latency (<100ms p99), circuit breakers via go-resiliency, retries with jitter, timeouts at 2s.'
Failure mode: 'Caller fails fast — no cascading blocking.'

CARD 2 — 'Asynchronous · Kafka / غير المتزامن'
Icon: stream
Accent: purple
Use cases:
• OrderCreated → Notification, Analytics, Loyalty (fan-out)
• DriverLocation → Geo Service, Command Center (high-volume stream)
• MenuUpdated → Catalog, Search (eventual consistency OK)
Properties: 'Throughput 50K+ msgs/sec, retention 7-30 days, Avro schemas, exactly-once for payments.'
Failure mode: 'Consumer lag — alert if >1000 msgs behind.'

CARD 3 — 'Real-time · WebSocket / الوقت الحقيقي'
Icon: wifi_tethering
Accent: success-green
Use cases:
• Customer App — order tracking (driver location every 5s)
• Driver App — new order pushes (sub-second)
• Command Center — live ops dashboard
Properties: 'WS Gateway service, 100K concurrent connections per pod, horizontal scaling via Redis pub/sub.'
Failure mode: 'Client auto-reconnects with exponential backoff; missed events fetched via REST.'

Bottom note (small): 'Decision rule: if the caller NEEDS the result to proceed → gRPC. If the caller just announces something happened → Kafka. If clients need push → WebSocket.'

Slide header '04 — MICROSERVICES BREAKDOWN' and page '18 / 50'.

slides.title: API Gateway & Edge
slides.task_brief:
API Gateway and edge layer slide.

Title: 'API Gateway & Edge Layer / بوابة الـ API وطبقة الحافة'
Kicker: 'EDGE'
Subtitle: 'Single entry point for all 7 client apps — handling auth, rate limiting, routing, and TLS.'

Two-column layout:

LEFT — 'Capabilities / القدرات' (5 items in a list):

1. Authentication & Authorization
   'Validates JWT on every request, injects user_id and roles into upstream headers. Supports API keys for restaurant-to-platform integrations.'

2. Rate Limiting
   'Per-IP and per-user limits — 100 RPS default, 1000 RPS for restaurant API keys. Burst capacity for peak hours. Backed by Redis token bucket.'

3. Routing & Versioning
   'Path-based routing to 12 backend services. Supports /v1/, /v2/ versioning for backwards compatibility. Canary deployments via weighted routing.'

4. TLS Termination & WAF
   'TLS 1.3 termination at the edge. Cloudflare WAF rules for OWASP top 10. Bot detection for fake order attempts.'

5. Request Aggregation
   'BFF (Backend-For-Frontend) pattern — Customer App\'s /home endpoint aggregates restaurant list + promotions + user profile in one call. Reduces client round trips from 5 to 1.'

RIGHT — 'Components / المكونات' (3 cards):

• Kong API Gateway (or APISIX)
  'Open-source, Lua plugins for custom logic, 30K RPS per node. Deployed as 3-node HA cluster behind NLB.'

• WebSocket Gateway (custom Go service)

slides.title: Database Architecture
slides.task_brief:
Database architecture overview slide.

Title: 'Database Architecture / معمارية قاعدة البيانات'
Kicker: 'POSTGRESQL · MULTI-TENANT'
Subtitle: '12 service-owned databases — polyglot persistence tuned per domain.'

Top section — 'Database-per-Service / قاعدة لكل خدمة' (text card):
'Each of the 12 microservices owns its own PostgreSQL logical database. No service reads another\'s database directly — only via API or event. This enforces domain boundaries and lets each service optimize its schema independently. Cross-service joins are replaced by either API composition (for reads) or event-carried state transfer (for writes).'

Middle — 4 storage tiers in a row:

TIER 1 — 'Primary OLTP / المعاملات'
• PostgreSQL 16 (RDS or self-managed on EC2)
• 12 logical databases, one per service
• Read replicas (1 primary + 2 read replicas per critical service)
• Failover via Patroni

TIER 2 — 'Geospatial / الجغرافية'
• PostgreSQL + PostGIS extension
• Used by Restaurant Catalog + Delivery Matching
• For sub-ms queries: Redis GEO (GEOADD, GEORADIUS)
• Driver location stream: Redis only (50K writes/sec)

TIER 3 — 'Cache / التخزين المؤقت'
• Redis 7 cluster (6 nodes, 3 shards + replicas)
• Session, hot menu data, driver availability, rate limits
• TTL-based eviction + explicit invalidation on Kafka events

TIER 4 — 'Search & Analytics / البحث والتحليلات'
• ElasticSearch 8 (restaurant/menu search, full-text Arabic)
• ClickHouse (analytics aggregations, 100M+ rows/day)
• S3 / MinIO (images, documents, audit logs)

Bottom — 'Partitioning Strategy / استراتيجية التقسيم':
'Orders table partitioned by month (range partitioning) — keeps active partitions small. Driver_locations archived to S3 after 7 days. Restaurant data sharded by city for >10K restaurants — Cairo/Giza/Alexandria/Mansoura each get a shard.'

Slide header '05 — DATABASE SCHEMA' and page '21 / 50'.

slides.title: Sharding & Scaling Strategy
slides.task_brief:
Database sharding and scaling strategy slide.

Title: 'Sharding & Scaling Strategy / استراتيجية التقسيم والتوسع'
Kicker: 'SCALE-OUT'
Subtitle: 'How the database layer scales from 500 to 50K orders/day without rewrites.'

Three growth stages shown as horizontal cards with arrows between them:

STAGE 1 — 'MVP (500 orders/day) / مرحلة الإنطلاق'
Single PostgreSQL instance:
• 1 primary + 1 read replica
• 16 vCPU, 64GB RAM, 1TB NVMe
• All 12 databases on one cluster
• Backup: daily snapshot + WAL streaming
• Estimated cost: $400/month

STAGE 2 — 'Growth (5K orders/day) / مرحلة النمو'
Vertical + horizontal split:
• Critical services (Order, Payment, Driver) on dedicated clusters
• Read replicas added (2 per critical cluster)
• Redis cluster scaled to 6 nodes
• Kafka cluster: 3 brokers
• Patroni for HA failover
• Estimated cost: $2,500/month

STAGE 3 — 'Scale (50K orders/day) / مرحلة التوسع'
Full sharding:
• Orders sharded by city (Cairo/Giza/Alex shards)
• Driver_locations moved to Redis-only + S3 archive
• ClickHouse added for analytics (offload from Postgres)
• Read replicas: 5 per critical cluster
• PgBouncer connection pooling (max 200 conns/service)
• Kafka: 5 brokers, 30+ partitions per topic
• Estimated cost: $12,000/month

Bottom note (small): 'Decision rule: shard only when a single primary can\'t keep up with write throughput (typically >5K writes/sec). Premature sharding adds complexity without benefit.'

slides.title: Order Lifecycle State Machine
slides.task_brief:
Order lifecycle state machine (CSS-built with cards + arrows).

Title: 'Order Lifecycle State Machine / دورة حياة الطلب'
Kicker: 'STATE MACHINE'
Subtitle: '8 states, 12 valid transitions — every status change audited and event-published.'

Layout: A horizontal flow of 8 state cards with arrow divs between them. Some states have alternative paths shown below.

Main flow (top row):

[1] PENDING → [2] CONFIRMED → [3] PREPARING → [4] READY → [5] PICKED_UP → [6] DELIVERED

Branches below:

From PENDING:
→ CANCELLED (customer cancelled within 60s, no charge)
→ BLOCKED (fraud score >80, refund issued)

From CONFIRMED:
→ CANCELLED (restaurant rejected, full refund)

From PREPARING/READY:
→ CANCELLED (support-initiated, partial refund based on prep stage)

From PICKED_UP:
→ CANCELLED (driver emergency, restaurant re-makes or refunds)

Each state card has:
- State name (bold, monospace)
- Color tag matching severity (cyan for normal, amber for at-risk, red for terminal failure)
- One-line description
- Trigger event (e.g., 'Trigger: OrderCreated event')

Bottom note: 'Every transition emits a Kafka event (order.status_changed) consumed by Notification, Analytics, and Loyalty services. State machine enforced at the Order Service layer — direct DB updates are forbidden.'

Slide header '06 — BUSINESS LOGIC FLOWS' and page '26 / 50'.

slides.title: Payment Flow
slides.task_brief:
Payment capture flow diagram.

Title: 'Payment Flow — Capture & Refund / تدفق الدفع'
Kicker: 'PAYMENTS'
Subtitle: 'Vodafone Cash, InstaPay, Card, COD — unified under one idempotent Payment Service.'

Two side-by-side flow diagrams:

LEFT — 'Payment Capture / عملية الدفع' (vertical flow):

1. Customer taps 'Place Order'
2. Order Service creates PENDING order
3. Order Service → Payment Service (gRPC, idempotency_key=order_id)
4. Payment Service selects provider:
   - Vodafone Cash for EGP <500
   - InstaPay for EGP 500-2000
   - Card for >EGP 2000 or recurring customers
5. Payment Service calls provider API (with 30s timeout)
6. Provider redirects customer to auth (OTP/biometric)
7. On success → Payment Service publishes PaymentCaptured
8. Order Service consumes → updates order to CONFIRMED
9. Notification Service pushes to Restaurant App

Failure handling:
- Timeout → Payment Service marks as PENDING_TIMEOUT, retries once
- Provider decline → PaymentFailed event, Order cancelled
- Duplicate idempotency_key → returns original result (no double charge)

RIGHT — 'Refund Flow / عملية الاسترجاع' (vertical flow):

1. Customer requests cancellation OR Support initiates
2. Order Service validates cancellation policy:
   - Before CONFIRMED: 100% refund
   - Before PREPARING: 100% refund
   - During PREPARING: 50% refund
   - After READY: 0% refund (restaurant cost already incurred)
3. Order Service publishes OrderCancelled
4. Payment Service consumes → calls provider refund API
5. Refund may take 1-5 business days (provider-dependent)
6. Payment Service publishes PaymentRefunded
7. Notification pushes to customer with refund reference
8. Loyalty Service reverses any points earned

slides.title: Delivery Matching Algorithm
slides.task_brief:
Delivery matching algorithm explanation slide.

Title: 'Driver Matching Algorithm / خوارزمية مطابقة السائق'
Kicker: 'DISPATCH ENGINE'
Subtitle: 'Finding the best driver in under 10 seconds — geospatial + scoring + batching.'

Three-column layout:

COLUMN 1 — 'Step 1: Geospatial Filter / الفلترة الجغرافية'
Icon: my_location
'When RestaurantAccepted fires, the Delivery Matching Service queries Redis GEO for all available drivers within 3km of the restaurant. Uses GEORADIUS (or GEOSEARCH in Redis 6+). Returns driver IDs sorted by distance. Typical result: 15-40 candidates in dense areas, 3-8 in suburban areas. The 3km radius is configurable per city — tuned based on traffic density.'

Key code snippet (monospace, small):
```
GEOSEARCH drivers AVAILABLE
  FROMLONLAT lng lat
  BYRADIUS 3 km
  ASC COUNT 40
```

COLUMN 2 — 'Step 2: Scoring / التقييم'
Icon: tuning
'Each candidate gets a score (0-100) computed from 5 weighted factors:'

• Distance to restaurant (35%)
  Closer = higher score. Linear decay beyond 1.5km.

• Driver rating (20%)
  Customer rating 4.5+ → 100; <4.0 → 50.

• Recent acceptance rate (20%)
  Last 20 orders acceptance rate. Penalizes drivers who reject often.

• Idle time (15%)
  Time since last delivery. Favors drivers who\'ve been waiting longer (fairness).

• Vehicle type match (10%)
  Motorcycle preferred for short distances; car for orders >10km or rain.

COLUMN 3 — 'Step 3: Dispatch & Retry / الإرسال وإعادة المحاولة'
Icon: send
'Top 3 candidates receive push notifications simultaneously (broadcast strategy). First to accept wins. If no acceptance within 30s, expand radius to 5km and retry with next 5 candidates. After 3 failed rounds (90s total), order moves to manual dispatch queue in Command Center.'

Properties:
• Target: 90% of orders matched within 10s
• P95 matching time: <15s
• Auto-recovery: 3 retries before manual
• Driver fair-share: rotated to prevent one driver from monopolizing

slides.title: Three-Layer Fraud Defense
slides.task_brief:
Three-layer fraud defense slide.

Title: 'Three-Layer Fraud Defense / ثلاث طبقات دفاعية'
Kicker: 'DEFENSE IN DEPTH'
Subtitle: 'Customer fraud, driver fraud, internal fraud — each with dedicated detection and response.'

Three large layer cards stacked vertically (or side by side if space allows):

LAYER 1 — 'Customer Fraud / احتيال العملاء'
Icon: person_off (danger red accent)
Threats:
• Fake accounts for promo abuse (new user coupons)
• Stolen credit cards (chargeback risk)
• Friendly fraud (claim non-delivery to get free food)
• COD abuse (order, refuse at door, food wasted)
Detection:
• Device fingerprinting (FingerprintJS)
• Phone number age + verification
• Order velocity per device/IP/household
• ML model trained on historical chargebacks
• Trust score (0-100) on every user, recalculated on each order
Response: 'Block auto-refund for users with trust score <30; require OTP for high-value COD orders.'

LAYER 2 — 'Driver Fraud / احتيال المندوبين'
Icon: two_wheeler (warning amber accent)
Threats:
• Fake deliveries (mark delivered without actually delivering)
• Collusion with restaurants (split overcharging)
• GPS spoofing to game matching
• Multi-accounting (one person, multiple driver accounts)
• Long-hauling (taking longer routes to inflate distance pay)
Detection:
• GPS trajectory validation (compare actual route to optimal)
• Customer delivery confirmation (photo + OTP)
• Restaurant pickup confirmation scan
• Cross-driver restaurant frequency analysis
• Driver earnings anomaly detection
Response: 'Auto-suspend drivers with >3 flagged orders in 7 days; manual review by Field Supervisor.'

LAYER 3 — 'Internal Employee Fraud / احتيال الموظفين الداخليين'
Icon: badge (accent purple)
Threats:
• Manual refunds for friends/family
• Approving fake restaurant signups for kickbacks
• Leaking customer data
• Order manipulation (cancelling real orders to redirect)
Detection:
• Biometric auth (fingerprint/face) on every portal login + sensitive action
• Immutable audit log (append-only, blockchain-style hashing)
• Anomaly detection on refund volume per employee
• Segregation of duties (refund approval requires 2 people >EGP 500)
• Quarterly access review by Command Center
Response: 'Immediate account suspension; legal action per Egyptian cybercrime law (Law 175/2018).'

Slide header '07 — SECURITY & ANTI-FRAUD' and page '32 / 50'.

Left column — 'Portal Modules / وحدات البوابة':

1. Employee Onboarding

3. Audit Log (Immutable)

4. Dual Approval Workflow
   'High-value actions require 2 approvers from different teams. Refund >EGP 500 → support_l2 + ops_manager. Restaurant onboarding → ops_manager + finance. Driver KYC approval → field_supervisor + ops_manager.'

5. Quarterly Access Review

CARD 2 — 'PCI-DSS for Card Payments / أمن بطاقات الدفع'
Icon: credit_card
• Never store full PAN (card number) — only last 4 digits + token
• Payment tokenization via provider (Stripe/Paymob)
• All card input fields rendered in provider\'s iframe (out of our scope)
• Quarterly ASV scans, annual SAQ-A submission
• Network segmentation: payment subnet isolated
Status: 'PCI-DSS SAQ-A — lowest validation tier because we never touch raw card data.'

CARD 3 — 'Customer Data Protection / حماية بيانات العملاء'
Icon: privacy_tip
• PII encryption at rest (column-level for phone, email, address)
• Data retention: 7 years for financial records (tax law), 3 years for orders, 90 days for driver GPS
• Right to deletion: customer can request account deletion via Support
• Data export: GDPR-style data portability (JSON export within 30 days)
• No PII shared with third parties without explicit consent
Status: 'Privacy policy published; consent flows implemented at signup.'

CARD 4 — 'Audit Log Retention / احتفاظ بسجل التدقيق'
Icon: history
• Hot tier: 90 days in PostgreSQL (fast query)
• Warm tier: 1 year in S3 + Parquet (queryable via Athena)
• Cold tier: 7 years in S3 Glacier (compliance retention)
• Hash chain validated nightly — alerts on tampering
• Logs immutable — even super_admin cannot modify
Status: 'Retention policy enforced via automated lifecycle rules; legal hold capability for active investigations.'

CARD 4 — 'Observability'

CARD 5 — 'Secrets & Config'

Scaling strategy for peak loads slide.

Title: 'Scaling Strategy — Peak Load Handling / استراتيجية التوسع'
Kicker: 'SCALE'
Subtitle: 'How the platform handles 5x spikes during lunch/dinner peaks without degradation.'

Left column — 'Peak Profile / ملف الذروة':

A small horizontal bar chart showing daily order volume by hour (CSS-built with simple divs):

Hour | Volume
12pm | ████████████████ 100% (peak)
1pm  | ██████████████  85%
2pm  | ████████        50%
3pm  | ███             20%
7pm  | ██████████████  85%
8pm  | ████████████████ 100% (peak)
9pm  | █████████████   75%
10pm | ████████        50%
11pm | ████            30%

Note: 'Lunch and dinner peaks carry 70% of daily volume. Provisioning for peak = provisioning for ~3x average.'

Right column — 'Scaling Tactics / تكتيكات التوسع' (5 cards):

1. Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA)
• Scale on CPU >70% AND RPS >threshold per service
• Min replicas: 3 (HA); Max: 50 per service
• Scale-up fast (<30s); scale-down slow (10min grace)

2. KEDA Event-Driven Autoscaling
• Kafka lag-based scaling for Notification, Analytics
• If consumer lag >1000 → add pods
• Critical for handling burst order events

3. Read Replicas
• Read traffic split: 80% to replicas, 20% to primary
• PgBouncer pools connections per service
• Replica lag monitored; alerts if >5s

4. Caching Strategy
• Redis for hot reads: restaurant list, menu items, promos
• TTL: 60s for restaurant list, 300s for menus, 30s for promos
• Cache invalidation on Kafka events (MenuUpdated, PromoCreated)
• Hit rate target: >85%

5. Pre-Scaled Capacity
• Pre-scale Order + Payment services 15 min before expected peak (cron-triggered)
• Based on historical data + day-of-week + weather
• Avoids cold-start latency during the first wave of orders

Technology decisions rationale slide — ADR-style summary.

Title: 'Technology Decisions — Rationale / مبررات القرارات التقنية'
Kicker: 'ADR SUMMARY'
Subtitle: 'Why each major tech choice was made — alternatives considered, decision, revisit triggers.'

6 decision cards (ADR-style) in a 3x2 grid. Each card has:
- Decision title
- Alternatives considered (small)
- Choice (highlighted)
- Why (1-2 sentences)
- Revisit trigger

1. Backend Language: Go
Alternatives: Node.js, Python, Java, Rust
Choice: Go 1.22
Why: 'Best concurrency model for I/O-heavy services; fast compile times enable rapid iteration; small binary size = fast container starts; strong standard library for HTTP/gRPC.'
Revisit: 'If we add ML inference to backend → Python sidecar for ML; Go stays for business logic.'

2. Frontend Framework: React (Native + Web)
Alternatives: Vue, Svelte, Flutter, Swift+Kotlin native
Choice: React 18 + React Native 0.74
Why: 'Single component mental model across mobile + web; largest talent pool in Egypt; ecosystem maturity; React knowledge reusable.'
Revisit: 'If performance becomes an issue on low-end Android → consider Flutter for Customer App only.'

3. Database: PostgreSQL
Alternatives: MySQL, MongoDB, DynamoDB
Choice: PostgreSQL 16 + PostGIS
Why: 'ACID guarantees for orders/payments; JSONB for flexible metadata; PostGIS for geospatial; mature ecosystem; battle-tested at scale (Uber, Instagram).'
Revisit: 'Never for transactional data. May add ClickHouse for analytics (planned).'

4. Event Bus: Apache Kafka
Alternatives: RabbitMQ, NATS, Redis Streams, AWS Kinesis
Choice: Apache Kafka 3.x (MSK or self-managed)
Why: 'Industry standard for event-driven; replay capability for fraud analytics; exactly-once semantics for payments; massive scale proven (LinkedIn, Uber, Netflix).'
Revisit: 'If operational burden too high → consider Upstash Kafka (managed) or Redpanda (Kafka-compatible, lower ops).'

5. Container Orchestration: Kubernetes
Alternatives: ECS, Nomad, Docker Swarm, serverless (Lambda)
Choice: Kubernetes (EKS)
Why: 'Portability (no vendor lock-in); rich ecosystem (Helm, ArgoCD, KEDA); standard skill for engineers; horizontal pod autoscaling critical for peak hours.'
Revisit: 'If team too small to operate K8s → ECS Fargate for first 6 months, migrate to EKS in Phase 2.'

6. Mobile Architecture: React Native
Alternatives: Native (Swift+Kotlin), Flutter, PWA
Choice: React Native 0.74 (New Architecture enabled)
Why: 'Single codebase for iOS+Android; same engineers can ship to both; fast iteration via hot reload; reusable components with web app; Hermes engine performance now comparable to native for our use case.'
Revisit: 'If driver app performance issues (GPS, background tasks) → consider going native for Driver App only.'
